description.task: Implement a transaction manager that supports commit and rollback. Demonstrate what happens when a multi-step operation fails halfway through — with and without rollback.
instructions: Implement rollback() to restore each account's balance from the snapshot map and print 'Transaction rolled back.'.
Verify the first transfer succeeds (commit) and the second reverts (rollback leaves balances unchanged).
description.hint: Spring's @Transactional wraps methods in transactions automatically. On RuntimeException, it rolls back changes. This ensures data consistency even when failures occur.
description.expectedOutput:
--- Successful Transfer ---
Transaction started.
Deducting $200.0 from Alice
Crediting $200.0 to Bob
Transaction committed.
Alice: $800.0 | Bob: $700.0
--- Failed Transfer (insufficient funds) ---
Transaction started.
Deducting $1000.0 from Bob
ERROR: Insufficient funds!
Transaction rolled back.
Alice: $800.0 | Bob: $700.0

description.task: Implement a custom @Transactional annotation processor that wraps annotated methods in try-commit/catch-rollback blocks using Java Reflection and dynamic proxies.
instructions: In the InvocationHandler lambda, get the actual method from OrderServiceImpl using method.getName() and target.getClass().getMethod().
If the real method has @Transactional, wrap invocation in begin/commit/rollback.
description.hint: Spring's @Transactional uses AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) with JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB to intercept method calls and manage transactions transparently.
description.expectedOutput:
[TX] BEGIN
Creating order for: Laptop
[TX] COMMIT
Order created for Laptop
[TX] BEGIN
Creating order for: Phone
[TX] ROLLBACK
Caught: Payment failed!
TX log: [BEGIN, COMMIT, BEGIN, ROLLBACK]

description.task: Simulate the four transaction isolation levels by showing what data each level can see from a concurrent 'transaction'. Implement a simple scenario demonstrating Dirty Read, Non-Repeatable Read, and Phantom Read.
description.context: Database isolation levels (READ_UNCOMMITTED, READ_COMMITTED, REPEATABLE_READ, SERIALIZABLE) control what changes from concurrent transactions are visible. Spring's @Transactional(isolation=...) sets these.
instructions: Implement readUncommitted() to return uncommittedValue (sees dirty data).
Implement readCommitted() to return storedValue (only committed data).
Verify the output shows the difference between isolation levels.
description.hint: In Spring: @Transactional(isolation = Isolation.READ_COMMITTED) is the most common choice. PostgreSQL's default is READ_COMMITTED. SERIALIZABLE is the safest but slowest.
description.expectedOutput:
Initial value: 100
[TX-B] Updated value to 999 (NOT committed yet)
[READ_UNCOMMITTED] TX-A reads: 999
[READ_COMMITTED] TX-A reads: 100
[TX-B] Rolled back! Value remains: 100
After rollback:
[READ_UNCOMMITTED] TX-A reads: 100
[READ_COMMITTED] TX-A reads: 100

Isolation Levels Summary:
READ_UNCOMMITTED: Sees dirty (uncommitted) changes - fastest, least safe
READ_COMMITTED: Only sees committed changes - prevents dirty reads
REPEATABLE_READ: Same query returns same results within transaction
SERIALIZABLE: Full isolation - prevents phantom reads
